questions.text: Define molarity of a solution.
questions.expected_answer: Molarity is the number of moles of solute dissolved per liter of solution.

questions.text: Why is molality preferred over molarity for temperature-dependent studies?
questions.expected_answer: Molality depends on mass, which does not change with temperature, unlike volume used in molarity.

questions.text: State Henry's law.
questions.expected_answer: The partial pressure of a gas above a solution is proportional to its mole fraction in the solution.

questions.text: Give one application of Henry's law.
questions.expected_answer: Carbonated beverages are bottled under high pressure to increase the solubility of carbon dioxide.

questions.text: What are the conditions for an ideal solution?
questions.expected_answer: It obeys Raoult's law over all concentrations and has zero enthalpy and volume of mixing.

questions.text: What are colligative properties?
questions.expected_answer: Colligative properties depend on the number of solute particles, not their chemical nature.

questions.text: Define ebullioscopic constant.
questions.expected_answer: It is the elevation in boiling point produced by dissolving one mole of non-volatile solute in one kilogram of solvent.

questions.text: Why does adding salt to ice lower its melting point?
questions.expected_answer: Salt lowers the freezing point by reducing the chemical potential of the liquid phase.

questions.text: What is osmotic pressure?
questions.expected_answer: Osmotic pressure is the pressure needed to stop osmosis through a semipermeable membrane.

questions.text: Explain reverse osmosis.
questions.expected_answer: Reverse osmosis occurs when pressure greater than osmotic pressure forces solvent from solution to pure solvent side.

questions.text: What is a galvanic cell?
questions.expected_answer: A galvanic cell converts chemical energy of a spontaneous redox reaction into electrical energy.

questions.text: State the function of a salt bridge in a galvanic cell.
questions.expected_answer: It completes the circuit and maintains electrical neutrality in the half-cells.

questions.text: Define standard electrode potential.
questions.expected_answer: It is the electrode potential measured relative to the standard hydrogen electrode under standard conditions.

questions.text: How is the standard hydrogen electrode constructed?
questions.expected_answer: It uses platinum black electrode in 1 M acid with hydrogen gas bubbled at 1 bar pressure.

questions.text: What does a positive standard electrode potential indicate?
questions.expected_answer: It indicates a greater tendency for reduction than the standard hydrogen electrode.

questions.text: What is the significance of the Nernst equation?
questions.expected_answer: It relates electrode potential or cell emf to concentration of reacting species and temperature.

questions.text: State Faraday's first law of electrolysis.
questions.expected_answer: The mass deposited at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed.

questions.text: Define molar conductivity.
questions.expected_answer: Molar conductivity is the conductance of all ions produced by one mole of electrolyte in solution.

questions.text: Why does molar conductivity of weak electrolytes increase steeply on dilution?
questions.expected_answer: Dilution increases degree of dissociation, producing more ions.

questions.text: State Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions.
questions.expected_answer: Limiting molar conductivity equals the sum of individual limiting ionic conductivities of cations and anions.

questions.text: Define rate of a chemical reaction.
questions.expected_answer: It is the change in concentration of a reactant or product per unit time.

questions.text: What is average rate of reaction?
questions.expected_answer: Average rate is concentration change divided by the time interval over which it occurs.

questions.text: How does instantaneous rate differ from average rate?
questions.expected_answer: Instantaneous rate is measured at a particular moment, while average rate is over a finite interval.

questions.text: List factors that influence reaction rate.
questions.expected_answer: Concentration, temperature, catalyst, surface area, and nature of reactants affect reaction rate.

questions.text: Define rate law.
questions.expected_answer: Rate law is an experimentally determined relation between reaction rate and concentrations of reactants.

questions.text: What is order of a chemical reaction?
questions.expected_answer: Order is the sum of powers of concentration terms in the rate law.

questions.text: Define molecularity of a reaction.
questions.expected_answer: Molecularity is the number of reacting species colliding in an elementary step.

questions.text: Why can molecularity never be zero or fractional?
questions.expected_answer: It counts actual reacting molecules in an elementary event, so it must be a positive integer.

questions.text: What are pseudo first-order reactions?
questions.expected_answer: They are higher order reactions that behave as first order when one reactant is in large excess.

questions.text: Give the unit of rate constant for a zero-order reaction.
questions.expected_answer: The unit is mol L^-1 s^-1.

questions.text: What is a ligand in a coordination compound?
questions.expected_answer: A ligand is an ion or molecule that donates electron pairs to a central metal atom or ion.

questions.text: Define coordination number.
questions.expected_answer: Coordination number is the number of ligand donor atoms directly bonded to the central metal ion.

questions.text: What is a chelate ligand?
questions.expected_answer: A chelate ligand is a polydentate ligand that binds through two or more donor atoms forming a ring.

questions.text: Explain ambidentate ligand.
questions.expected_answer: An ambidentate ligand can coordinate through two different donor atoms.

questions.text: What is oxidation number of the central metal atom?
questions.expected_answer: It is the charge the metal would carry if ligands were removed with their shared electron pairs.

questions.text: What is linkage isomerism?
questions.expected_answer: Linkage isomerism occurs when an ambidentate ligand binds through different donor atoms.

questions.text: Define coordination isomerism.
questions.expected_answer: It occurs when ligands are interchanged between cationic and anionic complex ions.

questions.text: What causes color in transition metal complexes according to crystal field theory?
questions.expected_answer: Color arises from d-d transitions caused by absorption of visible light between split d orbitals.

questions.text: Define spectrochemical series.
questions.expected_answer: It is an arrangement of ligands in increasing order of crystal field splitting strength.

questions.text: Why are low-spin complexes generally diamagnetic?
questions.expected_answer: Strong field ligands pair electrons in lower orbitals, leaving no unpaired electrons.

questions.text: What are haloalkanes?
questions.expected_answer: Haloalkanes are aliphatic hydrocarbons in which one or more hydrogen atoms are replaced by halogens.

questions.text: Why do haloalkanes have higher boiling points than parent alkanes?
questions.expected_answer: Their polar carbon-halogen bonds create stronger intermolecular attractions.

questions.text: State Markownikoff's rule.
questions.expected_answer: In addition to an unsymmetrical alkene, the negative part of reagent attaches to carbon with fewer hydrogen atoms.

questions.text: What happens in Finkelstein reaction?
questions.expected_answer: Alkyl chlorides or bromides react with sodium iodide in dry acetone to form alkyl iodides.

questions.text: Why does boiling point decrease with branching in isomeric haloalkanes?
questions.expected_answer: Branching reduces surface area and weakens van der Waals forces.

questions.text: What is a nucleophilic substitution reaction?
questions.expected_answer: It is a reaction in which a nucleophile replaces another leaving group in a molecule.

questions.text: Distinguish between SN1 and SN2 mechanisms by steps.
questions.expected_answer: SN1 occurs in two steps through carbocation formation, while SN2 is a one-step concerted reaction.

questions.text: Why are haloarenes less reactive towards nucleophilic substitution?
questions.expected_answer: Resonance gives partial double bond character to carbon-halogen bond and the aryl carbon is sp2 hybridized.

questions.text: What is chirality?
questions.expected_answer: Chirality is the property of a molecule being non-superimposable on its mirror image.

questions.text: Define racemic mixture.
questions.expected_answer: A racemic mixture contains equal amounts of two enantiomers and is optically inactive by external compensation.
